question: Explain why traditional data processing tools are often inadequate for IoT-generated big data and name two fundamental characteristics of big data that contribute to this challenge.
expectedPoints: Volume: IoT devices generate a massive and continuous stream of data that overwhelms traditional databases.
Velocity: Data is produced at high speed, requiring real-time or near-real-time processing and analytics.
Variety: IoT data comes in diverse formats (sensor readings, logs, video) which are often unstructured or semi-structured.

question: Describe the primary function of a stream processing framework in an IoT architecture and provide one specific example of such a technology.
expectedPoints: Its primary function is to process data in motion, analyzing data streams in real-time as they are generated, rather than after they are stored.
This enables immediate insights, anomaly detection, and automated responses to live events from IoT sensors.
An example is Apache Kafka (for data ingestion and streaming) or Apache Flink/Apache Storm (for real-time computation and analytics).

question: Compare and contrast the roles of a data lake and a data warehouse within a comprehensive IoT big data analytics platform. Discuss the type of data each holds, its structure, and its primary analytical use case.
expectedPoints: Data Lake: Holds raw, unprocessed data in its native format (structured, semi-structured, unstructured). It is schema-on-read, optimized for storage scalability, and used for exploratory analysis, machine learning, and storing vast IoT data streams.

Data Warehouse: Holds processed, refined, and structured data. It is schema-on-write, optimized for complex query performance, and used for business intelligence, reporting, and historical trend analysis based on cleansed data.
Contrast: The data lake is the initial landing zone for all IoT data, while the data warehouse contains a curated subset of that data prepared for specific business queries.
